In this lesson, specifically, we're going to take a closer look at seller financing. We talked about seller financing in some of the other financing lessons, but I want to go deeper and explain why it's so important in a leveraged buyout deal. I'll show you some examples of seller financing in action and how to ensure a win-win for you and the seller. Let's get started. We'll begin by going over what seller financing is exactly. The basic idea of seller financing is that you're going to pay off the business in the future and these are fixed guaranteed payments to the seller over time. After the deal is already closed, you're going to be using the free cash flow that the business generates to pay the seller. In other words, the profit of the business generates the money that goes to the seller for you buying their business. Seller financing isn't only convenient for the buyer, it can also be a very tax-adventacious way to the seller as well, especially if you're buying the shares. The payments are spread out over a certain period of time, which spreads out the tax liability over multiple years. And if you're targeting businesses with owners who are already trying to get out, you're offering continued steady profits and payments for them. Ultimately, that can give you an edge with the seller. The owner no longer wants to work and you're giving them an out. They can go play golf, spend time with their family, go on vacations, they can do whatever they want to do and they have a continuous stream of income for a period of time. Depending on how the deal is structured, you might pay the seller some money down, then you make additional payments that happen annually, quarterly, monthly, however you structure the deal. And you as the buyer take over the business without paying upfront by leveraging the current cash flow and any future cash flow you generate. Let's take a step back and look at the overall structure of a leveraged buyout. We're going to be breaking up the deal into three sections. The first section, the existing debt that you'll be inheriting as part of the deal, if there is any. The second, the closing payment, third, and then the future payments that you're making to the seller over time. The future payments might make up the whole deal, the whole cost of the business, meaning the entirety of the deal is covered through seller financing. Or the future payments are part of the total amount of the deal, which means you've secured financing for some amount and the seller is financing you for the additional amount. There's two kinds of seller financing. The one we're talking about in this lesson are the fixed guaranteed payments that are paid over time, either monthly, annually, or whatever you've negotiated. And then there are called what's earnouts and I'll cover those in detail in the next lesson. Not every seller will want seller financing as part of the deal. It really depends on the type of owner that you're working with. Someone who is looking for cash flow now won't want the seller financing option. But if you're targeting the distressed business owner or someone who's wanting to retire, these are perfect structures for them. It allows them to continue cash flow over a period of time without them having to work for it. For those business owners that are looking for continued income streams, the odds are they'll have a better chance of getting them to accept seller financing as part of the deal. And that's really who you should be targeting. Business owners who are flexible because they want out, they're burned out, maybe they want to retire or maybe there's a death or something in the family and they just need somebody to take the business over. You might be able to find someone who accepts some portion of seller financing as part of the deal, but they'll still want some amount of money up front usually. For example, they might accept seller financing for 25% of the deal, so you pay the seller 25% of the payment over some period of time and you'll still need to finance the 75% up front. On the flip side, if the seller has some condition that they're willing to finance the whole business, you might be able to leverage 80, 90 or even 100% seller financing. Once the seller is ready to move forward with the financing, we need to consider the terms of the deal. With seller financing, you're looking at paying an interest premium to the seller over time. That might be somewhere in the ballpark of 5% to 10% depending on how aggressive you negotiate. For the seller, that's great. This makes it a seller financing an even more attractive option. For you, the buyer, the financing will sit on your balance sheet as a liability as a seller note. If you're looking at leveraging an SBA loan as a financing option, at least 5% of the purchase price of the company has to remain unpaid as a standby note until the SBA loan has been repaid in full. So if you're introducing a seller note, 5% of the purchase price must be incorporated in that seller note. And the seller must be on standby for the whole length of the SBA loan. That means at least 5% of the purchase price won't be in the seller's hands until the SBA loan is repaid. They may not like that, by the way. Just something to consider if you're looking at using both seller financing and an SBA loan. Next, we'll cover security. In most cases, a seller will ask for some form of security as part of the deal. In a perfect world for us, there's no security involved in the deal. The seller is happy to take the deal on faith. They trust you that you're going to make the payments. But most often, a seller will want some additional reassurance from the purchaser. There are additional options too if you can get a deal done that way. For example, one of the options is a seller may hold on to their shares and release them to you as they get paid and use those shares for collateral. Let's say you do a 100% seller financing, but you can only put 50% down. The seller will only give you 50% of their shares if you do a stock purchase. And as you make payments to the seller, you start receiving more shares over time. So you start by owning 50% of the business. The additional 50% is held in escrow as collateral and as you pay over time, they're released to you. In some cases, the seller may put a lien on your assets in the business as well to secure their shares. This is called a debenture. This means that the seller could claim possession and ownership of those assets if you do not make payments. But it's also important to know if those assets are already used to secure another form of financing for the closing payment, you can't use those assets. Those are already claimed. It works the other way too. If the seller has charge over the business assets, they cannot be used for any asset based financing or SBA loans. And for those financing options, it's incredibly important that those assets are freed up and available. This is why we ideally only want to see this type of deal with sellers using equity instead of assets. In the next lesson, we're going to cover another form of financing through what's called seller earnouts. With earnouts, you're paying the seller, but it's conditioned upon the success of the business and what criteria you establish for that. So I'll see you in the next lesson where we cover this in depth. I'll see you there.
